subject: Chemistry
title: Avogadro's Number: Counting Atoms
description: A 60-second lesson on Avogadro's Number: Counting Atoms.

hook.text: One mole of any substance contains exactly 602,214,076,000,000,000,000,000 particles. That's Avogadro's number.

buildup.text: In 1811, Avogadro proposed that equal volumes of gas at the same temperature and pressure hold equal numbers of particles.

discovery.text: This meant you could count atoms by weighing them. One mole of carbon weighs 12 grams and contains 6.022 × 10²³ atoms.

twist.text: Avogadro never knew his number. It was calculated 50 years after his death by Johann Loschmidt.

climax.text: If you counted one atom per second, it would take 19 quadrillion years to count one mole. That's a big number.

punchline.text: A bridge between the atomic and human scale. Weigh to count.
